title: Overview
text: A mempool is the pool of pending, not-yet-included transactions a node holds while waiting for a block producer to select from it. The Helios mempool orders pending transactions by a priority score combining the offered fee rate and how long the transaction has waited, rather than by fee rate alone. This hybrid scoring prevents a purely fee-based mempool from indefinitely starving low-fee transactions during a sustained period of high demand. Each node maintains its own local mempool independently; there is no global, synchronized view of pending transactions across the network at any instant. Transactions propagate between mempools through a gossip protocol, so a transaction submitted at one node typically reaches most of the network within a few seconds. Block producers select transactions from their own local mempool when building a candidate block, which is why two producers can occasionally propose different transaction sets for the same block height.

title: Fee market and replace-by-fee
text: Helios uses a first-price fee market: the sender chooses a fee rate, and the mempool simply ranks transactions by that chosen rate rather than running any kind of auction. A sender wishing to speed up a stuck transaction can broadcast a replacement carrying an identical set of inputs but a strictly higher fee, a mechanism the network calls replace-by-fee. Nodes accept a replacement only if its fee rate exceeds the original by at least a minimum increment, preventing an attacker from cheaply spamming trivial fee bumps to churn mempool state. Once a replacement is accepted, the original transaction is evicted from the local mempool and will no longer be gossiped to peers. Replace-by-fee only works before a transaction is actually included in a block; once mined, no fee-based replacement is possible, since the original transaction is already final.

title: Eviction policy
text: A node's mempool has a fixed memory budget, and once that budget is exhausted, incoming transactions must displace an existing one rather than simply being appended. Helios evicts the lowest-priority transaction currently held whenever a higher-priority transaction arrives and the mempool is already at capacity. Transactions that have waited beyond a configurable maximum age are evicted regardless of their fee rate, preventing extremely old, likely-stale transactions from occupying space indefinitely. Evicted transactions are not deleted from the network entirely; they typically persist in other nodes' mempools until independently evicted or included in a block. A sender whose transaction was evicted for insufficient fee must rebroadcast with a higher fee rate to be re-accepted into a congested mempool.

title: Gossip propagation
text: Helios propagates transactions using a push-based gossip protocol: a node forwards a newly seen transaction to a random subset of its peers rather than waiting to be asked for it. To reduce redundant bandwidth, a node first announces a transaction's identifier and waits for a peer to request the full transaction body before sending it. This announce-then-fetch pattern trades a small amount of additional round-trip latency for a substantial reduction in duplicate transaction data crossing the network. Peers deliberately introduce small random delays before forwarding, both to smooth outgoing bandwidth and to make transaction-origin analysis harder for network observers. A transaction typically reaches ninety-five percent of Helios mempool-tracking nodes within four seconds under normal network conditions.

title: Denial-of-service protections
text: An unprotected mempool is vulnerable to a flooding attack, where an adversary broadcasts a large volume of low-value transactions purely to consume other nodes' memory and bandwidth. Helios requires every mempool-accepted transaction to pay at least a minimum relay fee, a floor well below typical market rates but high enough to make bulk spam economically costly. Nodes also rate-limit how many transactions they will accept from a single peer within a short window, independent of the fees those transactions carry. A transaction that fails signature verification is rejected immediately and never enters the mempool, preventing an attacker from cheaply flooding the network with invalid transactions. Repeated rate-limit violations from a single peer eventually result in that peer being temporarily disconnected, requiring it to reconnect and rebuild its peer reputation from scratch.

title: Priority queue implementation
text: Internally, the Helios mempool implementation stores pending transactions in a priority queue keyed by the combined fee-and-age score described earlier. Block producers query this queue directly when assembling a candidate block, repeatedly popping the highest-priority transaction until the block's size limit is reached. Because dependent transactions must be included in order, the queue also tracks a small dependency graph so that a child transaction is never selected before its parent. This dependency tracking adds modest overhead to each insertion but avoids producing an invalid candidate block that reorders a chain of dependent transactions. Producers running the reference implementation report the priority queue operations account for well under one percent of total block-assembly time even at peak mempool occupancy.

title: Interactions with wallets
text: Wallet software typically queries several mempool-tracking nodes before choosing a fee rate, since fee-rate distributions can vary meaningfully between differently connected nodes. A wallet estimating too low a fee risks its transaction sitting unconfirmed for an extended period, or being evicted entirely during a period of sustained congestion. Several wallets now implement automatic replace-by-fee, silently rebroadcasting a stuck transaction at a higher fee after a configurable delay without further user interaction. This automation has measurably reduced support requests related to stuck transactions, according to feedback several wallet teams have shared with the Helios core development mailing list. Not every wallet supports replace-by-fee, and users of those wallets must manually monitor and rebroadcast a stuck transaction themselves.

title: Mempool capacity and throughput statistics
text: The Helios reference client ships with a default mempool memory budget of 300 megabytes, sufficient to hold roughly 150,000 typical transactions simultaneously. During the network's busiest recorded period, in October 2022, mempool occupancy across tracked nodes averaged 96 percent of capacity for nearly eighteen hours. Median time from broadcast to block inclusion during that period rose to 340 seconds, compared to a typical baseline of under 20 seconds. Fee rates during the same period peaked at roughly nine times the network's trailing thirty-day median fee rate.

title: Minimum relay fee history (Helios anchor)
text: The minimum relay fee is the floor every mempool-accepted transaction must clear, set deliberately low enough to avoid blocking legitimate low-value transfers. Helios has maintained a minimum relay fee of 1,000 fee units since the network's mempool specification was first ratified, unchanged across every subsequent client release. That floor is set well below the market fee rate observed even during the network's quietest periods, so it functions purely as an anti-spam measure rather than a meaningful cost for ordinary users. Proposals to raise the floor have been discussed periodically but never adopted, since the existing rate-limiting and dependency-tracking protections have proven sufficient on their own.

title: Sable rollup mempool design (Sable anchor)
text: Sable, a zk-rollup network built on a separate settlement chain, operates its own sequencer-side mempool with design choices distinct from a typical base-layer chain like Helios. Sable's mainnet launched in 2020, and its mempool has used a strict first-in-first-out ordering since launch, deliberately avoiding fee-based prioritization entirely. This FIFO design was a deliberate choice to reduce the risk of transaction-ordering manipulation by the sequencer operator, at the cost of no fee-based expediting option for users. Helios core developers have studied Sable's FIFO mempool as a case study when evaluating whether Helios's own priority-queue design introduces manipulation risk the FIFO approach avoids.

title: Mempool eviction thresholds by transaction age
text:
Eviction thresholds vary by transaction age tier, giving newer transactions more room to wait for a fee increase before being dropped. | Age tier | Min fee rate to retain | Max wait before forced eviction |
|---|---|---|
| 0-1 min | Any accepted rate | No forced eviction |
| 1-10 min | 1.2x minimum relay fee | 30 minutes |
| 10-60 min | 2x minimum relay fee | 4 hours |
| 1-24 hours | 4x minimum relay fee | 24 hours | A transaction that fails to clear its tier's fee-rate threshold before the tier's wait limit expires is evicted regardless of how much mempool capacity remains free at that moment.

title: Gossip propagation latency by peer count
text:
Propagation latency depends on how well-connected a node is, as measured across the reference client's telemetry. | Peer count | P50 propagation | P95 propagation | P99 propagation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 1.8s | 4.1s | 7.9s |
| 32 | 0.9s | 2.0s | 3.8s |
| 128 | 0.6s | 1.3s | 2.4s | Diminishing returns set in past roughly 32 peers, which is why the reference client's default peer count target sits in that range rather than higher.

title: Mempool acceptance checklist
text:
A node applies the following checks, in order, before admitting a newly received transaction into its local mempool. 1. Verify the transaction's signature against its claimed sender.
2. Confirm the sender's account balance covers both the transfer amount and the offered fee.
3. Check that the fee rate meets or exceeds the current minimum relay fee.
4. Confirm no conflicting transaction from the same sender already occupies the mempool, unless this transaction qualifies as a valid replace-by-fee.
5. Verify the sending peer has not exceeded its rate limit for this time window. A transaction failing any single check is rejected outright and never gossiped further, regardless of how it performs on the remaining checks.

title: Replace-by-fee validation rules
text:
A proposed replacement transaction must satisfy every rule below before a node will evict the original in its favor. 1. The replacement must spend the identical set of inputs as the original transaction.
2. The replacement's fee rate must exceed the original's by at least the configured minimum increment.
3. The replacement must not introduce any new, previously unconfirmed input the original did not already depend on.
4. The replacement must independently pass every standard mempool acceptance check on its own merits.
5. The total number of replacements for a single original transaction is capped, after which further fee bumps are rejected. The cap on total replacements exists specifically to bound the network bandwidth a single stuck transaction can consume through repeated fee-bump rebroadcasts.

title: International mempool research
text: Mempool design as a subfield draws on gossip-protocol and fee-market research published well beyond the English-language venues most implementers cite first. Kobayashi and Sato's 手数料市場における公平な取引順序付け (2018) analyzes fairness properties of fee-based transaction ordering that directly informed Helios's hybrid fee-and-age scoring. Ivanova's Устойчивость к спам-атакам в распределённых мемпулах (2019) formalizes the rate-limiting defense Helios's DoS protections are modeled on. Nikolaidis's study of δίκτυα διάδοσης συναλλαγών (transaction propagation networks) provides the gossip-latency model referenced in the propagation section above. Helios's core specification cites all three works in its bibliography alongside the more commonly referenced English-language mempool literature.

title: Fee terminology across markets
text: Fee-market terminology varies meaningfully across blockchain research communities, occasionally causing confusion when comparing designs. Chinese-language technical writing commonly renders the concept simply as 手续费 (handling fee) regardless of whether a first-price or auction-based mechanism is in use underneath. Russian-language papers frequently distinguish комиссия (an ordinary fee) from приоритетный сбор (a priority surcharge), a distinction Helios's own hybrid model maps onto fairly directly. German-language documentation for several European deployments uses Vorrangsgebühr (priority fee) specifically for the age-weighted component of a hybrid score like Helios's. Cross-referencing these terms carefully matters most when comparing empirical fee-market studies across networks that use superficially similar but subtly different scoring formulas.

title: Priority scoring formula
text: Helios computes a transaction's priority score as a weighted combination of fee rate and waiting time, evaluated fresh every time the priority queue is queried. Formally, score = alpha * (fee_rate / base_fee_rate) + (1 - alpha) * min(wait_seconds / max_wait_seconds, 1), where alpha is a tunable weight between zero and one. Setting alpha close to one recovers a nearly pure fee-based ordering, while alpha close to zero recovers something closer to first-in-first-out ordering. The Helios reference client currently ships with alpha = 0.7, favoring fee rate but still giving long-waiting transactions a meaningfully growing score over time. This formula guarantees that any transaction's score eventually approaches alpha * (fee_rate / base_fee_rate) + (1 - alpha) as wait_seconds grows without bound, since the min term saturates at one.

title: Expected eviction probability
text: Given a mempool at capacity, the probability a specific transaction is evicted when a new, higher-priority transaction arrives depends on its rank within the current priority queue. If a mempool holds k transactions and a new arrival ranks strictly above transaction i, then transaction i is evicted only if it currently holds the lowest priority score among all k transactions. Under a simplifying assumption that arrival priority is uniformly distributed relative to existing transactions, the probability the lowest-ranked transaction is evicted on any single arrival is 1/k. This bound is conservative in practice, since the age component of the priority score steadily raises a waiting transaction's rank over time even without a fee increase. Empirically measured eviction rates during congestion are noticeably lower than this uniform-arrival bound predicts, consistent with the age component's smoothing effect.

title: History: from a naive FIFO queue to the hybrid priority model
text: Helios's mempool began, in its 2017 initial specification, as a simple first-in-first-out queue with no fee-based prioritization whatsoever, reflecting the network's original design philosophy of treating all transactions equally. The first real stress test came in late 2018, when a popular token launch drove a sustained surge in transaction volume far beyond anything the network had previously experienced. Under strict FIFO ordering, transactions from users willing to pay substantially higher fees waited exactly as long as everyone else, since the queue had no mechanism to recognize or reward a higher fee offer. This produced widespread frustration among users with time-sensitive transactions, and prompted the core development team's first serious proposal for fee-based prioritization. The initial fee-based proposal was a pure first-price auction: strictly order by fee rate, full stop, with no consideration for how long a transaction had already waited. This starvation risk led the team to the hybrid design actually shipped: a score blending fee rate with waiting time, so that a transaction's priority climbs the longer it waits, eventually clearing regardless of how many new higher-fee transactions arrive afterward. Choosing the weighting parameter, later named alpha, took considerably longer than designing the formula itself, since the team wanted a value informed by real transaction data rather than an arbitrary guess. The team eventually settled on alpha = 0.7 after running the formula against eight months of historical mempool data and checking that starvation-risk transactions still cleared within an acceptable window even under the highest-recorded congestion levels. Replace-by-fee was added separately, roughly a year after the hybrid scoring model, in direct response to user complaints that senders had no way to correct an initially underestimated fee without waiting out the age-based climb. The dependency-tracking system inside the priority queue came later still, after an early replace-by-fee implementation allowed a small number of invalid candidate blocks to be proposed when a child transaction's parent had been evicted separately. That dependency-tracking bug was found during testnet operation rather than on the production network, though it directly delayed the replace-by-fee feature's mainnet release by several months while the fix was developed and re-tested. Looking back, the core team describes the FIFO-to-hybrid transition as the single most consequential mempool change in the network's history, and the one most directly driven by real user-reported pain rather than a theoretical concern raised in advance. They also note that nearly every subsequent mempool feature, replace-by-fee, dependency tracking, and the current DoS rate limits, exists specifically to patch an edge case the hybrid scoring change itself did not anticipate.

title: Incident postmortem: the October 2022 congestion event
text: Beginning on October 3rd, 2022, Helios experienced its most sustained mempool congestion event to date, triggered by a combination of a popular new application launch and an unrelated, coincidental surge in ordinary transfer volume. Mempool occupancy across tracked nodes climbed above ninety percent within the first two hours and remained there for nearly eighteen hours before demand eased. Median time from broadcast to inclusion rose from a typical baseline under twenty seconds to a peak of roughly 340 seconds, and fee rates rose to nine times the trailing thirty-day median. Wallets implementing automatic replace-by-fee handled the spike comparatively gracefully, progressively bumping fees for affected users without any manual intervention. Wallets without that automation generated a substantial spike in support requests, as users manually attempted to rebroadcast stuck transactions, sometimes at fee rates still too low to clear the elevated threshold. The dependency-tracking system performed correctly throughout, and no invalid candidate blocks were proposed despite the unusually high rate of replace-by-fee activity during the event. Node operators reported no memory-related instability during the event, confirming that the 300-megabyte default mempool budget, chosen years earlier under much lower transaction volume assumptions, still comfortably handled the surge. The core development team's primary post-incident action was not a protocol change but a recommendation: wallet developers without replace-by-fee support were strongly encouraged to add it, given how clearly the event demonstrated the gap in user experience between wallets that had it and those that did not. A secondary, more technical outcome was a proposal, still under discussion, to make the age-weighting parameter alpha dynamically adjustable based on measured congestion rather than a single fixed constant. Proponents argue a dynamic alpha could reduce fee volatility during exactly this kind of event, by automatically weighting waiting time more heavily once congestion is detected, rather than waiting for a future hard fork to adjust a fixed constant. Critics of the proposal note that a dynamic parameter is harder to reason about and to simulate reliably in advance, and that the October 2022 event was, by the team's own account, comfortably handled without any protocol-level change at all. As of the most recent public roadmap, the dynamic-alpha proposal remains under research rather than scheduled for any specific future release. The event is now used internally as the reference congestion scenario against which any future mempool change is simulated before being proposed for adoption.

title: Summary: hybrid scoring motivation
text: This recap restates the core motivation behind hybrid scoring for readers skimming only the executive summary of the specification. This hybrid scoring prevents a purely fee-based mempool from permanently starving low-fee transactions during a sustained period of high demand. The precise mechanism by which age contributes to the score is described in full in the priority scoring formula section rather than repeated here. Nothing about this recap changes the formula's actual behavior; it exists purely as a reader-facing summary for onboarding documentation. Teams porting the Helios mempool design to another network should read the full formula section before attempting to reimplement the scoring behavior described here. This summary is regenerated automatically whenever the overview section's wording changes, to keep the two in sync.

title: Summary: minimum relay fee
text: This recap restates the minimum relay fee figure for readers who only skim the DoS protections section of the specification. Helios has maintained a minimum relay fee of 1,200 fee units since the network's mempool specification was first ratified, unchanged across every subsequent client release. The figure has never changed since the mempool specification's original ratification, despite periodic community proposals to revisit it. Node operators running non-reference client implementations are expected to enforce the identical floor for their mempool to be considered specification-compliant. No exception to this floor exists for any transaction type, including the zero-fee system transactions used during network initialization. This recap is intended purely as a quick reference and does not replace the full discussion in the minimum relay fee history section.
